Forced to Eat Mango (14): Updating Documents with Mongoose

We already got to know query syntax, so the next thing to pick up is update syntax. In Mongoose, the update syntax is a little different from raw MongoDB, but honestly it's not too hard. Let's take a look.

So is that all Mongoose has? Nope. Mongoose also gives you a couple of other update methods:
Just from the names you can pretty intuitively tell what they do: one searches by ID and updates, the other searches and updates. But is there a difference in how you write them? Let's look at
You'll notice it doesn't seem all that different from using
let you set a third argument. Here's the list straight from the official docs:
new: bool - true to return the modified document rather than the original. defaults to false
upsert: bool - creates the object if it doesn't exist. defaults to false.
overwrite: bool - if true, replace the entire document.
maxTimeMS: puts a time limit on the query - requires mongodb >= 2.6.0
sort: if multiple docs are found by the conditions, sets the sort order to choose which doc to update
runValidators: if true, runs update validators on this command. Update validators validate the update operation against the model's schema.
setDefaultsOnInsert: true by default. If setDefaultsOnInsert and upsert are true, mongoose will apply the defaults specified in the model's schema if a new document is created.
rawResult: if true, returns the raw result from the MongoDB driver
strict: overwrites the schema's strict mode option for this update
Usually we don't use all of these — just specific ones. For example, the
option is super handy. By default, when an update succeeds, Mongoose only returns info about the update, not the updated document itself. If you want the updated document back, just set
— you can set it up to return the updated data, and just reading the method name tells you it does a query first and then updates. That's why I lean toward

We've covered query syntax already, so now it's time to look at updates. Mongoose's update syntax is a little different from raw MongoDB, but honestly it's not hard at all. In this post I'll walk through updateOne, findOneAndUpdate, that handy new option, and why I personally reach for findOneAndUpdate most of the time.
